perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall take all reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or improper use of the Material, to prevent the material from falling into the public domain, and to protect it from falling into the possession of Third Parties. Such measures include, but shall not be limited to, the highest de gree of care that the Receiving Party utilises to protect its own confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall take commercially reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or improper use of the Material, to prevent the material from falling into the public domain, and to protect it from falling into the possession of Third Parties. Such measures include, but shall not be limited to, the degree of care that the Receiving Party utilises to protect its own confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'all reasonable measures' to 'commercially reasonable measures' weakens the standard of care. If Section 7, which discusses indemnity, refers back to this duty of care, it's now unclear whether the Receiving Party will be held to the 'highest degree of care' for indemnity purposes, or just 'commercially reasonable measures'. Because it's ambiguous on how to implement the Indemnity section, this makes the text have an internal contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7. Indemnity The Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that any breach of this Agreement would cause immediate and irreparable injury to the Consorti um and its Members. Should the Receiving Party violate any of the terms and conditions of this Agreement, the Consortium Members shall be entitled, in addition to any other remedies that maybe available, in law, in equity or otherwise, to obtain injunctive relief against the threatened br each of this Agreement or the continuation of any such breach, without the nece ssity of proving actual damages. In addition, in case of violation of this Agreemen t, the Receiving Party may be expelled from the Consortium in accordance with the conditions provided in Section 4 of the Consortium Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text specified 'all reasonable measures,' implying a higher standard of care for protecting confidential information. The changed text lowers this standard to 'commercially reasonable measures.' Section 7, the contradicted text, refers to breaches of the agreement, which now includes a weakened standard of care. It creates ambiguity regarding the level of care required from the Receiving Party in order to avoid triggering indemnity under section 7. This is a strong contradiction because the changed standard could lead to disputes about what constitutes a breach and the resulting consequences outlined in Section 7, therefore violating the definition of 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party may disclose th e Material if and to the exte nt that such disclosure is required by law or court order, provided that the Receiving Party notifies the Disclosing Page 2 of 5 LND99 448148-1.049002.0010 Molybdenum Consortium, First Amendment Jan 2008 Party. Any disclosure of Data and/or Studies that could result in public disclosure of the Data and/or Studies shall on ly be permissible after prio r approval from the Steering Committee or the IMOA Executive Committee.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party may disclose the Material if and to the extent that such disclosure is recommended by legal counsel or requested by court order, provided that the Receiving Party makes a good faith effort to notify the Disclosing Party. Any disclosure of Data and/or Studies that could result in public disclosure of the Data and/or Studies shall only be permissible after prior informal consultation with, but not necessarily approval from, the Steering Committee or the IMOA Executive Committee.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that disclosure is allowed if 'required by law or court order' after proper notification. The modified version allows disclosure if 'recommended by legal counsel or requested by court order,' but requires a 'good faith effort' at notification, rather than mandatory notification. Furthermore, it changes from 'prior approval' to 'prior informal consultation', reducing the hurdle for disclosure. This creates an ambiguity as to what constitutes a breach, making it contradict the indemnity clause that imposes consequences for breaches.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7. Indemnity The Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that any breach of this Agreement would cause immediate and irreparable injury to the Consorti um and its Members. Should the Receiving Party violate any of the terms and conditions of this Agreement, the Consortium Members shall be entitled, in addition to any other remedies that maybe available, in law, in equity or otherwise, to obtain injunctive relief against the threatened br each of this Agreement or the continuation of any such breach, without the nece ssity of proving actual damages. In addition, in case of violation of this Agreemen t, the Receiving Party may be expelled from the Consortium in accordance with the conditions provided in Section 4 of the Consortium Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the conditions under which disclosure is permissible, shifting from a strict requirement ('required by law or court order') to a recommendation ('recommended by legal counsel or requested by court order') and weakening the notification requirement. The prior approval condition has also been reduced to informal consultation. This creates a contradiction with Section 7, the indemnity clause, because it makes the definition of 'breach' ambiguous, as the text has softened the parameters that define it, directly impacting the enforceability of the agreement and remedies available for breach. The original text provided certainty, whereas the new text provides ambiguity. This meets the definition of Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall survive until the earlier of (i) such time as all the material disclosed hereunder becomes public ly known pursuant to Section 15 of the Consortium Agreement and made generally available without vi olation of this Agreement, or (i i) twelve (12) years from date of Registration.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall survive until the earlier of (i) such time as the majority of material disclosed hereunder becomes publicly known and readily available, or (ii) ten (10) years from the effective date of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement's term ends when 'all' material becomes public, and it lasts for twelve years from registration. The modified version changes this to when 'the majority' of material is public and 'readily available', and it reduces the duration to ten years from the 'effective date'. This in-text contradiction introduces ambiguity around how to measure when the agreement terminates, because the end date may be interpreted in different ways and can cause disputes on how to act on the material, making this contradict the Non-disclosure restriction stated in section 2.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. Non-disclosure and Restriction on Use The Receiving Party agrees to retain the Material in confidence a nd not to use the Material for any purpose other than in accordance with the terms of this Agreement and of the Consortium Agreement. In Particular, the Receiving Party undertakes (in addition and wit hout prejudice to any commitments under the Consortium Agreement), that: a. the Receiving Party shall not disclose the Material to any Third Party except with the Steering Committee’s prior written consent an d provided that no othe r legal disclosure requirement applies; b. the Material shall neither be copied, nor otherwise reproduced nor duplicated in whole or in part where such copying, reproduction or duplication have not been specifically authorized by the Consortium Agreement or otherwise approved in writing by the Steering Committee; The Receiving Party acknowledges that the covena nts of non-disclosure and non-use in this Agreement shall be effective in every country in the world. The Receiving Party shall take all reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or improper use of the Material, to prevent the material from falling into the public domain, and to protect it from falling into the possession of Third Parties. Such measures include, but shall not be limited to, the highest de gree of care that the Receiving Party utilises to protect its own confidential information. In the ev ent of loss or theft of any documents, items of work in progress, or any work products embodying Material, the Consortium Secretariat must be notified immediately by the Receiving Party, who shall cooperate fully in remedying same.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the conditions for the agreement's termination, changing 'all' material to 'the majority' and reducing the term from 12 to 10 years. This directly impacts the non-disclosure obligations outlined in section 2. Section 2 requires the Receiving Party to keep the Material in confidence and prevent it from falling into the public domain. However, with the modified text, the Receiving Party's obligations end when 'the majority' of the material is public, potentially conflicting with the non-disclosure restriction stated in section 2, because the end date may be interpreted in different ways and can cause disputes on how to act on the material, making this contradict the Non-disclosure restriction stated in section 2.

perturbation.original_text: No failure or delay by either party in exercising any right hereunder shall operate as a waiver thereof, nor shall any single or partial exercise thereof preclude any other or further exercise thereof or the exercise of any other right hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: A failure or delay by either party in exercising a right hereunder may operate as a waiver thereof at the sole discretion of the other party, and a single or partial exercise thereof may preclude any other or further exercise thereof or the exercise of any other right hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause is a 'no waiver' clause, meaning inaction doesn't waive rights. The modified version states that inaction *can* be a waiver at the *other party's discretion*. This creates a direct contradiction and makes it unclear whether a party's inaction should be interpreted as a waiver, which affects the possibility of an indemnity claim, in Section 7, making this an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7. Indemnity The Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that any breach of this Agreement would cause immediate and irreparable injury to the Consorti um and its Members. Should the Receiving Party violate any of the terms and conditions of this Agreement, the Consortium Members shall be entitled, in addition to any other remedies that maybe available, in law, in equity or otherwise, to obtain injunctive relief against the threatened br each of this Agreement or the continuation of any such breach, without the nece ssity of proving actual damages. In addition, in case of violation of this Agreemen t, the Receiving Party may be expelled from the Consortium in accordance with the conditions provided in Section 4 of the Consortium Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text contained a 'no waiver' clause, protecting a party's rights even if they delayed or failed to exercise them. The changed text introduces the possibility of waiver based on inaction at the other party's discretion. This contradicts the 'Indemnity' section (Section 7), because if a party's inaction now constitutes a waiver, it directly impacts their ability to claim indemnity for a breach, as that inaction might be construed as waiving their right to claim such indemnity. This is a strong in-text contradiction as it fundamentally alters the rights and obligations related to indemnity, making enforcement unclear.
